parameters.name: Crew metabolic energy requirement
parameters.source: NASA BVAD NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev2, Eq. 3-2 (EER) and Tables 4-57/4-61 (12.707 MJ/CM-d)
parameters.notes: 12.707 MJ/CM-day for the average astronaut via the Estimated Energy Requirement equation. Older CELSS studies use 2500-3000 kcal.

parameters.name: Prepackaged food mass, as-shipped (incl. intrinsic water, incl. packaging)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-47 (Perchonok 2002 lower; Douglas 2017 max 6-month rate upper)
parameters.notes: ISS flight value; 42-53% water content. Packaging is a separate line item (~0.27 kg/CM-d).

parameters.name: Food dry mass (reference)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-47 (MSIS 1995) and Table 4-60 (Levri)
parameters.notes: Dehydrated/dry basis, excludes intrinsic and rehydration water.

parameters.name: Food packaging mass
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-40 (Ewert 2017); upper from 2.36 kg total with 17% packaging
parameters.notes: Packaging is ~13-17% of prepackaged-food upmass; becomes trash mass.

parameters.name: Required prepackaged-food shelf life for Mars
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.5.1 (Cooper et al. 2011)
parameters.notes: Current ISS foods degrade (vitamin/quality loss) before 5 yr; a genuine open technology gap and a driver toward in-situ crops for multi-year stays.

parameters.name: Edible dry-matter caloric density (staple-crop average)
parameters.source: MELiSSA/Frontiers 2023 (avg of bread wheat, durum wheat, potato, soybean = 4000 kcal/kg dw)
parameters.notes: Staples (wheat/potato/rice) ~3.7-4.0; soybean ~4.9; peanut ~5.5; lettuce ~2.5-3.0. Ideal edible composition 70% carb / 20% protein / 10% lipid.

parameters.name: White potato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 best BPC (18.4; 20.8 with transplant); U. Wisconsin >30; BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 21.06
parameters.notes: Highest edible dry-mass productivity of any candidate crop; the calorie workhorse.

parameters.name: White potato radiation-use efficiency, edible biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.44 measured, 0.50 with transplant; U. Wisconsin >0.80)
parameters.notes: RUE_total 0.64. Sets lighting energy per calorie; use 0.44 (conservative) to 0.5-0.64 (optimistic).

parameters.name: White potato harvest index
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (0.70); Wheeler 2008 measured ~0.68; Tibbitts CELSS 0.80
parameters.notes: High HI is why potato dominates calorie-per-area and calorie-per-kWh.

parameters.name: Wheat (grain) edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 measured 11.3 (15.8 with VOC scrub to HI 0.40); BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 20.0
parameters.notes: BPC HI was depressed to 0.29 by ethylene; scrubbing VOCs recovers HI 0.40. Total-biomass productivity 39.6 g dw/m2-d.

parameters.name: Wheat radiation-use efficiency, edible (grain) biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.17 measured, 0.24 with VOC scrub, 0.30 potential); RUE_total 0.59
parameters.notes: Low edible RUE (grain HI 0.4) makes wheat ~2.6x more power-costly per calorie than potato under LEDs.

parameters.name: Wheat harvest index
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (0.40); Wheeler 2008 observed 0.29 (ethylene-depressed)
parameters.notes: Ethylene/VOC accumulation in closed chambers cut BPC wheat HI to 0.29; scrubbing restores ~0.40.

parameters.name: Sweet potato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-90 (HI 0.60)
parameters.notes: Highest BVAD edible productivity; DLI 28 mol/m2-d, 12 h photoperiod, 85 d cycle. Excellent calorie crop.

parameters.name: Soybean edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (6.0 measured, 6.9 with transplant); BVAD Table 4-90 nominal 4.54
parameters.notes: Primary protein/oil crop; RUE_edible only 0.16 g/mol so it is power-expensive but nutritionally essential.

parameters.name: Soybean radiation-use efficiency, edible biomass
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (0.16 measured, 0.19 transplant); RUE_total 0.43
parameters.notes: Lowest edible RUE of the staples; caloric density ~4.9 kcal/g dw partly offsets this.

parameters.name: Lettuce edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (7.1 measured, 11.8 with transplant, RUE 0.70); BVAD Table 4-90 6.57
parameters.notes: Fast 28-30 d cycle, HI 0.90, but ~2.5-3 kcal/g dw and mostly water; a morale/vitamin crop, not calories.

parameters.name: Tomato edible productivity (dry basis)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (9.8 measured, 11.4 transplant); BVAD Table 4-90 10.43; RUE_edible 0.25
parameters.notes: Salad crop; 94% water fresh; HI 0.45.

parameters.name: Crop transpiration / water uptake (mixed-crop average)
parameters.source: Wheeler et al. 2008 (3.96 avg condensed); BVAD Table 4-91 per-crop (lettuce 2.1 ... wheat 11.79)
parameters.notes: Recovered as near-distilled condensate on cooling coils; ~100% recyclable to nutrient solution or potable loop. Wheat is a large water user (11.8 L/m2-d).

parameters.name: LED horticultural photon efficacy (2026 planning value)
parameters.source: Kusuma, Pattison & Bugbee 2020 (ceiling 3.4 white+red / 4.1 blue+red); industry 2023-24 fixtures 2.5-3.5
parameters.notes: Diode electrical->photon efficiency: blue 0.93, white 0.76, red 0.81. Water/humidity optics cut ~10%.

parameters.name: Light delivery efficiency (PPF at canopy / PPF emitted)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-86 (Sager 1999 low; Sager 2006 high)
parameters.notes: Combine with efficacy: usable canopy PAR per electrical J = efficacy x delivery. BPC reflectors achieved only 0.10-0.15.

parameters.name: Electrical energy per mol PAR photons delivered to canopy
parameters.source: Derived: 1/(efficacy x delivery); efficacy 3.0 umol/J, delivery 0.8 (range from 2.5-3.5 umol/J)
parameters.notes: At LED output (delivery=1): 0.079-0.111 kWh/mol. This is the fundamental electricity-to-photons conversion.

parameters.name: LED electrical energy per food kcal, potato
parameters.source: Derived: 1/(RUE_edible x efficacy x delivery x caloric_density); potato RUE 0.44, 3.0 umol/J, 0.8, 4.0 kcal/g
parameters.notes: Lighting only, excludes ~25% pump/fan/thermal overhead. Optimistic 0.045 (RUE 0.64), conservative 0.075 (efficacy 2.5).

parameters.name: LED electrical energy per food kcal, wheat
parameters.source: Derived: wheat RUE_edible 0.17 (0.24 opt), efficacy 3.0 umol/J, delivery 0.8, 4.0 kcal/g
parameters.notes: ~2.6x potato's cost per calorie; drives the potato-over-grain design conclusion.

parameters.name: Plant growth chamber equivalent system mass per growing area
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Table 4-88 (Drysdale 1999b); lower bound = structure+root-zone excl. HPS lamps/ballasts
parameters.notes: Includes 20 kg/m2 crop structure + 36.8 kg/m2 root-zone water/nutrients + 31 kg/m2 HPS lamps+ballasts (LEDs lighter). Aeroponics reduces root-zone mass.

parameters.name: Fertilizer salt for full-diet crop production
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.14 (Lunn et al. 2017), for 2500 kcal/person/day full diet
parameters.notes: >50% recyclable from inedible biomass (composting/bioreactor) and processed urine; scales with closure fraction.

parameters.name: Growing area for 100% caloric closure (balanced diet)
parameters.source: Derived from Wheeler productivities (3037 kcal / 4 kcal/g / ~15 g edible dw/m2-d); consistent with CELSS/ICES literature
parameters.notes: Potato-only idealized ~41 m2; balanced nutrition (protein/oil crops) pushes to 40-60 m2. Excludes ~25-50% crew-access aisle area.

parameters.name: Electrical power for 100% caloric closure (balanced diet, LED)
parameters.source: Derived: 3037 kcal x ~0.10-0.17 kWh/kcal mix / 24.66 h x 1.25 overhead (LED 3.0 umol/J)
parameters.notes: Dominated by lighting. Potato-heavy mix lower (~8-10 kW); grain/legume-heavy higher. The single largest life-support power line item if food is grown.

parameters.name: Growing area, 50% calories from potato+wheat (worked example)
parameters.source: Derived (Sec. 8): Wheeler RUE/productivity; 27.1 m2 as-measured, 21.1 m2 optimized
parameters.notes: Potato ~9-10 m2, wheat ~12-17 m2. Wheat needs more area per calorie despite equal calorie split.

parameters.name: Electrical power, 50% calories from potato+wheat (worked example)
parameters.source: Derived (Sec. 8): 135 kWh/sol optimized to 224 kWh/sol as-measured, incl. +25% overhead; LED 3.0 umol/J
parameters.notes: Wheat contributes ~70% of the power despite half the calories. Range spans efficacy 2.5-3.5 umol/J and as-measured vs optimized crops.

parameters.name: Mars surface usable daily light integral (favorable latitudes)
parameters.source: NASA BVAD Sec. 4.14.1.1 (Clawson 2006)
parameters.notes: Mars receives ~43% of Earth's solar irradiance; up to ~30 mol/m2-d at some latitudes for much of the year, but dust storms can suppress it drastically. Enough for hybrid natural+LED but not sole-source for staples.

modeling_notes: Food splits into (1) resupplied prepackaged food (well-characterized ISS flight data: 1.83-2.39 kg/CM-day incl. ~0.27 kg packaging, 3037 kcal/CM-day demand, <=5 yr shelf life is an open gap) and (2) in-situ bioregenerative crops (soft numbers, all from NASA's Biomass Production Chamber 1987-1998). The crop trade is governed by radiation-use efficiency (RUE, g edible dw per mol PAR), harvest index, and LED photon efficacy. Lighting energy per calorie is set by 1/(RUE_edible x efficacy x delivery x caloric_density) and is INDEPENDENT of DLI (DLI only trades area vs. time). Central result: potato (high HI 0.70, RUE_edible 0.44) costs ~0.066 kWh/kcal vs. wheat ~0.17 and soybean ~0.18 -- potato/sweet-potato are ~3x cheaper per calorie, so a Mars menu should be tuber-biased with grain/legumes as nutrition supplements. Worked example (50% of one crew member's calories from potato+wheat): ~21-27 m2 and ~5.5-9 kW electrical (~135-224 kWh/sol), with wheat driving ~70% of the power. Full balanced-diet closure ~40-60 m2 and ~10-20 kW per person. CRITICAL CAUTIONS: (a) BVAD's chamber power of 2.6 kW/m2 is HPS-lamp-based and obsolete -- derive lighting from LED efficacy instead, this is the biggest error in legacy estimates; (b) BVAD Table 4-89 wheat light (115 mol/m2-d) is inconsistent with its own nominal yield -- use Wheeler RUE; (c) all BPC data are ~1 g Earth with elevated CO2 (1000-1200 ppm) and are optimistic upper bounds for Mars partial-gravity surface systems; (d) crop transpiration (~4 L/m2-d) is recovered as clean condensate and is a water-recovery benefit, not just a load. Fidelity tiers: L0 single scalars (Ybar~15 g edible dw/m2-d, ebar~0.10 kWh/kcal, ~45 m2 & ~10-15 kW per CM full closure); L1 per-crop analytic with edible_prod = RUE_edible x DLI x f_abs, staggered planting, seasonal Mars insolation, nutrient/water loops; L2 sub-sol physics (light-response photosynthesis, respiration, Penman-Monteith transpiration, coupled O2/CO2/water/thermal exchange with the habitat, stochastic pathogen/VOC/equipment failures on cycle-length recovery).
key_findings: Crew demand anchor: 3037 kcal/CM-day (12.707 MJ, BVAD EER); prepackaged food 1.83-2.39 kg/CM-day incl. ~0.27 kg/CM-day packaging (13-17% of upmass); >=5 yr shelf life is an unsolved technology gap driving in-situ production.

Potato is the calorie champion: highest edible productivity (18-21 g dw/m2-d), harvest index 0.70, RUE_edible 0.44 g/mol -> ~0.066 kWh(elec)/kcal, about 2.6x cheaper than wheat (0.17) and 3x cheaper than soybean (0.18) under LEDs.

Lighting energy per calorie = 1/(RUE_edible x LED_efficacy x delivery x caloric_density) and is INDEPENDENT of DLI; DLI only trades growing area against cycle time. This is the key structural insight for the model.

LED photon efficacy 2.5-3.0 umol/J is the 2026 deployable range (ceiling 3.4-4.1, Kusuma/Bugbee 2020) -> ~0.10-0.14 kWh(elec) per mol PAR at the canopy; this replaces BVAD's obsolete HPS-based 2.6 kW/m2 chamber-power figure.

Worked example (50% of one crew member's calories from potato+wheat): ~21-27 m2 growing area and ~5.5-9 kW electrical (~135-224 kWh/sol), with wheat responsible for ~70% of the power despite half the calories -- a potato-only mix would need ~18 m2 and ~4 kW.

Full balanced-diet closure lands at the classic ~40-60 m2 and ~10-20 kW per person; nutritional balance (protein/oil crops soybean/peanut, RUE ~0.16) is what makes 100% closure power-expensive, not the calories themselves.

Crop transpiration (~4 L/m2-d, up to 11.8 for wheat) is recovered as near-distilled condensate (~100% recyclable) -- a farm is also a water processor; but crops fail fast (Fusarium in ISS Veggie after a humidity excursion, ethylene cutting wheat HI 0.40->0.29), so never model >50-70% steady-state closure without a >=1-crop-cycle stored-food buffer.

Data-quality caveat: every crop productivity number traces to the KSC Biomass Production Chamber at ~1 g Earth gravity with elevated CO2 (1000-1200 ppm); treat as optimistic upper bounds for untested Mars 0.38 g crop-scale production.

sources.title: Life Support Baseline Values and Assumptions Document (BVAD), NASA/TP-2015-218570 Rev 2

sources.org: Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences
